clue: When his brother died, a French newspaper mixed them up and ran an obituary calling ___ the 'merchant of death' for inventing dynamite; horrified, he left his fortune to a peace prize.
This inventor fed pigeons for hours in New York parks and said of one favorite white pigeon, 'I loved that pigeon as a man loves a woman.'
This 16th-century Danish astronomer wore a metal prosthetic nose after a duel and kept a pet moose that reportedly died falling down the stairs after drinking too much beer.
Composer ___ wrote a short piano piece with the instruction to repeat it 840 times in a row, making performances last around 24 hours.
To study how we see color, this scientist slid a blunt needle between his own eyeball and the bone of his eye socket and pressed on it to see what appeared.
This ancient Greek philosopher ran a secretive cult that, among other rules, strictly forbade its members from eating beans.
Told that dogs were banned from his Cambridge college, this poet kept a pet bear in his rooms instead and joked it should sit for a fellowship, since no rule mentioned bears.
Paid a bonus for every world record he set, this pole vaulter broke the world record 35 times, often raising it by just one centimeter at a time.
This famous composer held an elaborate funeral, complete with veiled mourners and a graveside poem, for his dead pet starling.
This U.S. president kept a pet badger named Josiah in the White House, an animal his son cheerfully noted 'bites legs sometimes, but never faces.'
In 1859 this bankrupt San Francisco resident declared himself 'Emperor of these United States' and issued decrees abolishing Congress; locals humored him and even honored his self-printed currency.
This medieval king of France suffered the 'glass delusion,' convinced his body was made of glass, and had iron rods sewn into his clothing so he wouldn't shatter.
After this Romantic poet drowned and was cremated on an Italian beach in 1822, his heart refused to burn and was plucked from the ashes; his widow kept it wrapped in silk for decades.
To preserve her famous complexion, this 19th-century empress slept wearing a leather facial mask lined with raw veal.
This composer died in 1915 of blood poisoning after a pimple on his upper lip turned into a carbuncle and went septic.
